Intent — Definition.rule: Intent exists when a person acts with the purpose to cause a result or with knowledge that the result is substantially certain to occur.
Intent — Definition.application: Because the actor knew the result was substantially certain to occur, intent is established.

Transferred Intent.rule: Transferred intent applies when a defendant intends to commit an intentional tort against one person but causes a different intentional tort or harms a different person.
Transferred Intent.application: Because intent transfers between persons and torts, liability applies.

Battery.rule: Battery is the intentional infliction of harmful or offensive contact with another person without consent.
Battery.application: Because harmful contact was intentional and without consent, battery occurred.

Assault.rule: Assault occurs when a defendant intentionally causes reasonable apprehension of an imminent harmful or offensive contact.
Assault.application: Because the plaintiff reasonably feared imminent contact, assault occurred.

False Imprisonment.rule: False imprisonment occurs when a defendant intentionally confines another person without lawful privilege and without consent.
False Imprisonment.application: Because the plaintiff was confined without lawful authority, false imprisonment occurred.

Intentional Infliction of Emotional Dist.rule: Intentional infliction of emotional distress occurs when a defendant intentionally or recklessly engages in extreme and outrageous conduct causing severe emotional distress.
Intentional Infliction of Emotional Dist.application: Because the conduct was extreme and caused severe distress, IIED occurred.

Trespass to Land.rule: Trespass to land occurs when a defendant intentionally enters or causes a physical invasion of land possessed by another.
Trespass to Land.application: Because the defendant intentionally entered the land, trespass occurred.

Trespass to Chattels.rule: Trespass to chattels occurs when a defendant intentionally interferes with another’s personal property causing dispossession or actual harm.
Trespass to Chattels.application: Because the interference caused actual harm, trespass to chattels occurred.

Conversion.rule: Conversion occurs when a defendant intentionally exercises dominion or control over another’s personal property causing serious interference.
Conversion.application: Because the interference was serious, conversion occurred.

Consent — General Rule.rule: Consent is a defense to an intentional tort when the plaintiff voluntarily and knowingly agrees to the defendant’s conduct.
Consent — General Rule.application: Because the plaintiff knowingly agreed to the conduct, consent bars liability.

Consent — Express.rule: Express consent occurs when the plaintiff explicitly agrees to the defendant’s conduct through words or writing.
Consent — Express.application: Because the plaintiff explicitly agreed, express consent applies.

Consent — Implied.rule: Implied consent exists when the plaintiff’s conduct reasonably indicates consent to the defendant’s actions.
Consent — Implied.application: Because the plaintiff’s conduct indicated consent, implied consent applies.

Consent — Invalid Consent.rule: Consent is invalid if obtained through fraud, duress, or if the plaintiff lacks capacity to consent.
Consent — Invalid Consent.application: Because consent was obtained through fraud, it is invalid.

Self-Defense — General Rule.rule: Self-defense is justified when a person reasonably believes force is necessary to prevent imminent unlawful harm.
Self-Defense — General Rule.application: Because the defendant reasonably believed harm was imminent, self-defense applies.

Self-Defense — Proportional Force.rule: Self-defense permits only reasonable force and does not justify deadly force unless facing a threat of serious bodily harm.
Self-Defense — Proportional Force.application: Because the force used was proportional, self-defense is valid.

Defense of Others.rule: Defense of others is justified when the defendant reasonably believes that the person defended would have the right to use self-defense.
Defense of Others.application: Because the third party had a right to self-defense, the defense applies.

Defense of Property.rule: A person may use reasonable force to prevent or terminate an unlawful intrusion upon property but may not use deadly force.
Defense of Property.application: Because only reasonable force was used, defense of property applies.

Recapture of Chattels.rule: A person may use reasonable force to recapture chattels if in fresh pursuit of one who has wrongfully taken them.
Recapture of Chattels.application: Because the defendant acted in fresh pursuit, recapture was justified.

Necessity — Public.rule: Public necessity is a complete defense when the defendant acts to prevent a public disaster affecting the community.
Necessity — Public.application: Because the act prevented a public disaster, public necessity applies.

Necessity — Private.rule: Private necessity is a qualified defense that allows entry onto another’s land to prevent serious harm but requires payment for actual damage caused.
Necessity — Private.application: Because the defendant acted to prevent serious harm, private necessity applies but damages are owed.

Authority to Arrest.rule: A law enforcement officer or private citizen may assert authority to arrest as a defense when the arrest is lawful.
Authority to Arrest.application: Because the arrest was lawful, authority to arrest is a valid defense.

Negligence — Elements.rule: Negligence requires a duty of care, breach of that duty, causation, and damages.
Negligence — Elements.application: Because all four elements are present, negligence is established.

Duty — General Rule.rule: A person owes a duty to exercise reasonable care to avoid foreseeable risks of harm to others.
Duty — General Rule.application: Because the risk of harm was foreseeable, a duty of care existed.

Duty — To Whom (Foreseeable Plaintiffs).rule: A duty of care is owed only to foreseeable plaintiffs who are foreseeably at risk from the defendant’s conduct.
Duty — To Whom (Foreseeable Plaintiffs).application: Because the plaintiff was foreseeable, a duty was owed.

Duty — No Duty to Act.rule: There is no general duty to act or rescue another person absent a special relationship.
Duty — No Duty to Act.application: Because no special relationship existed, no duty to act arose.

Duty — Special Relationships.rule: A duty to act arises when a special relationship exists, such as common carrier–passenger, innkeeper–guest, employer–employee, or parent–child.
Duty — Special Relationships.application: Because a special relationship existed, a duty to act was imposed.

Duty — Undertaking.rule: A person who voluntarily undertakes to render services has a duty to act with reasonable care.
Duty — Undertaking.application: Because the defendant undertook to help, a duty of care arose.

Duty — Landowner to Trespassers.rule: A landowner owes trespassers a duty to refrain from willful or wanton misconduct.
Duty — Landowner to Trespassers.application: Because the defendant acted willfully, the duty was breached.

Duty — Landowner to Licensees.rule: A landowner owes licensees a duty to warn of known dangerous conditions not obvious to the licensee.
Duty — Landowner to Licensees.application: Because the danger was known and not obvious, a duty to warn existed.

Duty — Landowner to Invitees.rule: A landowner owes invitees a duty to use reasonable care to inspect, discover, and remedy dangerous conditions.
Duty — Landowner to Invitees.application: Because the landowner failed to inspect, the duty was breached.

Duty — Children on Land.rule: A landowner owes a duty of reasonable care to child trespassers when an artificial condition poses an unreasonable risk of harm.
Duty — Children on Land.application: Because the artificial condition posed an unreasonable risk, a duty was owed.

Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional.rule: A duty exists for negligent infliction of emotional distress when a defendant’s negligence causes foreseeable emotional harm.
Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional.application: Because emotional harm was foreseeable, a duty existed.

Standard of Care — Reasonable Person.rule: The standard of care is that of a reasonable person under the same or similar circumstances.
Standard of Care — Reasonable Person.application: Because the defendant failed to act as a reasonable person would, the standard was breached.

Standard of Care — Children.rule: A child is held to the standard of a reasonable child of the same age, intelligence, and experience unless engaged in an adult activity.
Standard of Care — Children.application: Because the child was engaged in an adult activity, the adult standard applied.

Standard of Care — Professionals.rule: A professional is held to the standard of care of a reasonably competent professional in the same field.
Standard of Care — Professionals.application: Because the professional failed to meet the professional standard, there was a breach.

Breach — Definition.rule: A breach occurs when a defendant’s conduct falls below the applicable standard of care.
Breach — Definition.application: Because the defendant’s conduct fell below the standard, a breach occurred.

Breach — Negligence Per Se.rule: Negligence per se applies when a defendant violates a statute designed to protect against the type of harm suffered by the plaintiff.
Breach — Negligence Per Se.application: Because the statute was violated and caused the harm, negligence per se applies.

Res Ipsa Loquitur.rule: Res ipsa loquitur permits an inference of negligence when the accident ordinarily does not occur without negligence and the instrumentality was under the defendant’s control.
Res Ipsa Loquitur.application: Because the accident would not occur absent negligence, res ipsa applies.

Causation — Actual Cause.rule: Actual cause requires that the defendant’s conduct be a cause in fact of the plaintiff’s injury.
Causation — Actual Cause.application: Because the injury would not have occurred without the defendant’s conduct, actual cause is established.

But-For Causation.rule: But-for causation is satisfied when the plaintiff’s injury would not have occurred but for the defendant’s conduct.
But-For Causation.application: Because the harm would not have occurred but for the defendant’s act, causation exists.

Multiple Sufficient Causes.rule: When multiple acts are each sufficient to cause the injury, each act is an actual cause of the harm.
Multiple Sufficient Causes.application: Because each act alone could have caused the harm, each is an actual cause.

Causation — Proximate Cause.rule: Proximate cause limits liability to harms that are a foreseeable result of the defendant’s conduct.
Causation — Proximate Cause.application: Because the harm was foreseeable, proximate cause is satisfied.

Direct Cause Test.rule: Under the direct cause test, the defendant is liable if the harm results directly from the defendant’s conduct without an intervening cause.
Direct Cause Test.application: Because the harm followed directly from the conduct, proximate cause exists.

Foreseeability Test.rule: Under the foreseeability test, proximate cause exists if the type of harm suffered was foreseeable.
Foreseeability Test.application: Because the type of harm was foreseeable, proximate cause is established.

Intervening Cause.rule: An intervening cause is an event that occurs after the defendant’s conduct and contributes to the plaintiff’s injury.
Intervening Cause.application: Because the later event contributed to the harm, it is an intervening cause.

Superseding Cause.rule: A superseding cause breaks the chain of causation when the intervening act is unforeseeable and relieves the defendant of liability.
Superseding Cause.application: Because the intervening act was unforeseeable, it was a superseding cause.

Eggshell Plaintiff Rule.rule: Under the eggshell plaintiff rule, a defendant is liable for all injuries caused, even if the plaintiff is unusually vulnerable.
Eggshell Plaintiff Rule.application: Because the defendant takes the plaintiff as found, liability extends to all injuries.

Damages — Requirement.rule: Negligence requires proof of actual damages to be actionable.
Damages — Requirement.application: Because no actual damages were proven, negligence is not established.

Compensatory Damages.rule: Compensatory damages compensate the plaintiff for actual losses caused by the defendant’s negligence.
Compensatory Damages.application: Because the plaintiff suffered actual loss, compensatory damages apply.

Economic Damages.rule: Economic damages compensate for monetary losses such as medical expenses and lost wages.
Economic Damages.application: Because the plaintiff incurred medical expenses, economic damages are recoverable.

Non-Economic Damages.rule: Non-economic damages compensate for pain and suffering, emotional distress, and loss of enjoyment of life.
Non-Economic Damages.application: Because the plaintiff experienced pain and suffering, non-economic damages are appropriate.

Punitive Damages.rule: Punitive damages may be awarded when the defendant’s conduct is willful, wanton, or reckless.
Punitive Damages.application: Because the conduct was reckless, punitive damages may be awarded.

Wrongful Death.rule: Wrongful death allows recovery by statutory beneficiaries for damages resulting from a decedent’s death caused by negligence.
Wrongful Death.application: Because negligence caused the death, wrongful death damages apply.

Survival Actions.rule: A survival action allows the decedent’s estate to recover damages the decedent could have recovered if alive.
Survival Actions.application: Because the decedent could have sued while alive, the estate may recover.

Negligence Per Se — Definition.rule: Negligence per se applies when a defendant violates a statute that establishes a standard of care.
Negligence Per Se — Definition.application: Because the defendant violated a statute establishing the standard of care, negligence per se applies.

Negligence Per Se — Protected Class.rule: Negligence per se applies only if the plaintiff is within the class of persons the statute was designed to protect.
Negligence Per Se — Protected Class.application: Because the plaintiff was within the protected class, the statute applies.

Negligence Per Se — Type of Harm.rule: Negligence per se applies only if the harm suffered is the type of harm the statute was intended to prevent.
Negligence Per Se — Type of Harm.application: Because the injury was the type the statute sought to prevent, negligence per se is established.

Negligence Per Se — Excused Violation.rule: A statutory violation may be excused when compliance is impossible, more dangerous, or due to an emergency not caused by the defendant.
Negligence Per Se — Excused Violation.application: Because compliance would have been more dangerous, the violation is excused.

Duty — Common Carrier.rule: A common carrier owes passengers a duty of reasonable care under the circumstances.
Duty — Common Carrier.application: Because the plaintiff was a passenger, the carrier owed a duty of care.

Duty — Innkeeper.rule: An innkeeper owes guests a duty of reasonable care to protect against foreseeable harms.
Duty — Innkeeper.application: Because the harm was foreseeable, the innkeeper owed a duty.

Duty — Landlord.rule: A landlord owes a duty of reasonable care for common areas under the landlord’s control.
Duty — Landlord.application: Because the injury occurred in a common area, the landlord owed a duty.

Duty — Employer.rule: An employer owes employees a duty to provide a reasonably safe workplace.
Duty — Employer.application: Because the workplace was unsafe, the employer breached the duty.

Duty — Parent to Child.rule: A parent owes a duty to exercise reasonable care to control a child to prevent foreseeable harm.
Duty — Parent to Child.application: Because the harm was foreseeable, the parent owed a duty.

Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional.rule: A bystander may recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress when the bystander closely relates to the victim and contemporaneously perceives the injury.
Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional.application: Because the plaintiff perceived the injury and was closely related, recovery is permitted.

Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional.rule: A plaintiff may recover for negligent infliction of emotional distress when the plaintiff is within the zone of danger and suffers emotional distress.
Duty — Negligent Infliction of Emotional.application: Because the plaintiff was within the zone of danger, a duty exists.

Dram Shop Liability.rule: A commercial vendor may be liable for serving alcohol to an intoxicated person who later causes injury to a third party.
Dram Shop Liability.application: Because alcohol was served to an intoxicated person, dram shop liability may apply.

Social Host Liability.rule: A social host generally is not liable for serving alcohol to adult guests, absent a statute imposing liability.
Social Host Liability.application: Because no statute imposed liability, the social host is not liable.

Strict Liability — General Rule.rule: Strict liability applies when a defendant engages in certain activities and is liable for resulting harm regardless of the level of care exercised.
buzzwords: regardless of the level of care
Strict Liability — General Rule.application: Because strict liability applies, lack of negligence is not a defense.

Abnormally Dangerous Activities.rule: Strict liability applies to abnormally dangerous activities that create a foreseeable and highly significant risk of harm even when reasonable care is exercised.
Abnormally Dangerous Activities.application: Because the activity created a highly significant risk, strict liability applies.

Factors for Abnormally Dangerous Activit.rule: An activity is abnormally dangerous based on factors including high risk of harm, likelihood of great harm, inability to eliminate risk with reasonable care, uncommon usage, and inappropriateness of location.
Factors for Abnormally Dangerous Activit.application: Because multiple factors indicate abnormal danger, strict liability applies.

Scope of Risk.rule: Strict liability applies only to harm that results from the type of risk that makes the activity abnormally dangerous.
Scope of Risk.application: Because the harm resulted from the dangerous risk, liability applies.

Animals — Wild Animals.rule: An owner of a wild animal is strictly liable for harm caused by the animal’s dangerous propensities.
Animals — Wild Animals.application: Because the animal was wild, the owner is strictly liable.

Animals — Domestic Animals.rule: An owner of a domestic animal is strictly liable if the owner knew or should have known of the animal’s dangerous propensities.
Animals — Domestic Animals.application: Because the owner knew of the animal’s danger, strict liability applies.

Defenses to Strict Liability.rule: Comparative negligence is not a defense to strict liability, but assumption of risk is a defense.
Defenses to Strict Liability.application: Because the plaintiff assumed the risk, recovery is barred.

Trespassing Animals.rule: The owner of livestock is strictly liable for damage caused by trespassing animals.
Trespassing Animals.application: Because livestock trespassed and caused damage, strict liability applies.

Products Liability — General Rule.rule: Products liability imposes liability on manufacturers and sellers for injuries caused by defective products placed into the stream of commerce.
Products Liability — General Rule.application: Because the product was defective and caused injury, products liability applies.

Products Liability — Theories of Recover.rule: Products liability claims may be based on strict liability, negligence, or breach of warranty.
Products Liability — Theories of Recover.application: Because strict liability applies, proof of negligence is unnecessary.

Strict Products Liability — Elements.rule: Strict products liability requires that the product was defective, the defect existed when the product left the defendant’s control, and the defect caused injury.
Strict Products Liability — Elements.application: Because the defect existed at sale and caused injury, strict liability applies.

Manufacturing Defect.rule: A manufacturing defect exists when a product departs from its intended design even though all possible care was exercised.
Manufacturing Defect.application: Because the product deviated from its design, a manufacturing defect exists.

Design Defect — Risk-Utility Test.rule: A design defect exists when the foreseeable risks of harm could have been reduced or avoided by a reasonable alternative design.
Design Defect — Risk-Utility Test.application: Because a safer alternative design was available, the design was defective.

Design Defect — Consumer Expectation Tes.rule: A design defect exists when a product is more dangerous than an ordinary consumer would expect.
Design Defect — Consumer Expectation Tes.application: Because the product was more dangerous than expected, a design defect exists.

Failure to Warn — Defect.rule: A product is defective due to failure to warn when foreseeable risks of harm could have been reduced or avoided by adequate warnings.
Failure to Warn — Defect.application: Because no adequate warning was given, the product was defective.

Failure to Warn — Obvious Dangers.rule: There is no duty to warn of risks that are open and obvious to a reasonable user.
Failure to Warn — Obvious Dangers.application: Because the danger was obvious, no warning was required.

Causation in Products Liability.rule: The plaintiff must prove that the product defect was a cause in fact and proximate cause of the injury.
Causation in Products Liability.application: Because the defect directly caused the injury, causation is satisfied.

Plaintiffs in Products Liability.rule: Any foreseeable user, consumer, or bystander injured by a defective product may recover.
Plaintiffs in Products Liability.application: Because the plaintiff was a foreseeable user, recovery is permitted.

Defendants in Products Liability.rule: Manufacturers, wholesalers, retailers, and commercial sellers may be held liable for defective products.
Defendants in Products Liability.application: Because the seller placed the product in commerce, liability applies.

Defenses — Assumption of Risk.rule: Assumption of risk is a defense to products liability when the plaintiff knowingly and voluntarily encounters the risk.
Defenses — Assumption of Risk.application: Because the plaintiff knowingly assumed the risk, recovery is barred.

Defenses — Comparative Fault.rule: Comparative fault may reduce recovery in products liability when the plaintiff’s misuse or negligence contributes to the injury.
Defenses — Comparative Fault.application: Because the plaintiff misused the product, damages are reduced.

Unforeseeable Misuse.rule: A defendant is not liable for injuries caused by an unforeseeable misuse of a product.
Unforeseeable Misuse.application: Because the misuse was unforeseeable, liability does not attach.

Breach of Warranty — Express.rule: An express warranty is created by an affirmation of fact or promise relating to the product that becomes part of the basis of the bargain.
Breach of Warranty — Express.application: Because the seller made a factual promise, an express warranty exists.

Breach of Warranty — Implied Warranty of.rule: The implied warranty of merchantability requires that goods be fit for the ordinary purposes for which such goods are used.
Breach of Warranty — Implied Warranty of.application: Because the product was unfit for ordinary use, the warranty was breached.

Breach of Warranty — Implied Warranty of.rule: An implied warranty of fitness for a particular purpose arises when the seller knows the buyer’s purpose and reliance.
Breach of Warranty — Implied Warranty of.application: Because the buyer relied on the seller’s judgment, the warranty applies.

Defamation — Definition.rule: Defamation is a false statement of fact concerning the plaintiff that is published to a third party and harms the plaintiff’s reputation.
Defamation — Definition.application: Because a false statement was published and harmed reputation, defamation is established.

Defamation — Libel and Slander.rule: Libel is defamation in written or recorded form, while slander is defamation in spoken form.
Defamation — Libel and Slander.application: Because the statement was written, it constitutes libel.

Defamation — Publication.rule: Publication occurs when the defamatory statement is communicated to any third person who understands it.
Defamation — Publication.application: Because a third party heard the statement, publication occurred.

Defamation — Identification.rule: The defamatory statement must be of and concerning the plaintiff.
Defamation — Identification.application: Because the statement referred to the plaintiff, identification is satisfied.

Defamation — Public Figure or Public Off.rule: A public figure or public official must prove actual malice, meaning knowledge of falsity or reckless disregard for the truth.
Defamation — Public Figure or Public Off.application: Because actual malice was proven, the public figure may recover.

Defamation — Private Figure.rule: A private figure must prove negligence regarding the truth or falsity of the statement to recover for defamation.
Defamation — Private Figure.application: Because the defendant was negligent, the private figure may recover.

Defamation — Defamation Per Se.rule: Defamation per se involves statements concerning crime, loathsome disease, professional incompetence, or sexual misconduct.
Defamation — Defamation Per Se.application: Because the statement alleged criminal conduct, defamation per se applies.

Defamation — Damages.rule: In defamation per se cases, damages are presumed, while in other cases the plaintiff must prove actual damages.
Defamation — Damages.application: Because the statement was defamation per se, damages are presumed.

Defamation — Truth as a Defense.rule: Truth is an absolute defense to defamation.
Defamation — Truth as a Defense.application: Because the statement was true, defamation liability is barred.

Defamation — Privileges.rule: Absolute or qualified privileges may protect a defendant from defamation liability.
Defamation — Privileges.application: Because the statement was privileged, liability is barred.

Invasion of Privacy — Overview.rule: Invasion of privacy consists of four distinct torts: intrusion upon seclusion, appropriation, false light, and public disclosure of private facts.
Invasion of Privacy — Overview.application: Because the conduct fits one of the four categories, invasion of privacy applies.

Intrusion Upon Seclusion.rule: Intrusion upon seclusion occurs when a defendant intentionally intrudes upon a plaintiff’s private affairs in a highly offensive manner.
Intrusion Upon Seclusion.application: Because the intrusion was highly offensive, liability applies.

Appropriation of Name or Likeness.rule: Appropriation occurs when a defendant uses a plaintiff’s name or likeness for commercial advantage without consent.
Appropriation of Name or Likeness.application: Because the name was used commercially without consent, appropriation occurred.

False Light.rule: False light occurs when a defendant publicly attributes false information to the plaintiff that would be highly offensive to a reasonable person.
False Light.application: Because false information was publicized, false light applies.

Public Disclosure of Private Facts.rule: Public disclosure of private facts occurs when a defendant publicly discloses private information that would be highly offensive and is not of legitimate public concern.
Public Disclosure of Private Facts.application: Because private facts were publicly disclosed, liability applies.

Intentional Misrepresentation (Fraud).rule: Intentional misrepresentation occurs when a defendant knowingly makes a false statement of material fact with intent to induce reliance and the plaintiff justifiably relies and suffers damages.
Intentional Misrepresentation (Fraud).application: Because the defendant knowingly made a false statement that induced reliance, fraud is established.

Negligent Misrepresentation.rule: Negligent misrepresentation occurs when a defendant makes a false statement of material fact without reasonable care and the plaintiff justifiably relies and suffers damages.
Negligent Misrepresentation.application: Because the defendant failed to exercise reasonable care, negligent misrepresentation applies.

Intentional Interference with Contract.rule: Intentional interference with contract occurs when a defendant intentionally induces a third party to breach an existing contract causing damages.
Intentional Interference with Contract.application: Because the defendant induced a breach of contract, liability applies.

Intentional Interference with Prospectiv.rule: Intentional interference with prospective economic advantage occurs when a defendant intentionally interferes with an expected economic relationship through wrongful conduct.
Intentional Interference with Prospectiv.application: Because wrongful conduct disrupted the expected relationship, liability applies.

Elements — Interference Torts.rule: Interference torts require intentional conduct, knowledge of the relationship, causation, and damages.
Elements — Interference Torts.application: Because all elements are present, the interference tort is established.

Defenses — Justification or Privilege.rule: Justification or privilege is a defense when the defendant acts to protect a legitimate economic interest using lawful means.
Defenses — Justification or Privilege.application: Because the defendant acted to protect a legitimate interest, the conduct is privileged.

Nuisance — Definition.rule: Nuisance is an unreasonable interference with the use and enjoyment of land.
Nuisance — Definition.application: Because the interference was unreasonable, a nuisance exists.

Private Nuisance.rule: Private nuisance occurs when a defendant substantially and unreasonably interferes with a private individual’s use or enjoyment of land.
Private Nuisance.application: Because the interference substantially affected the plaintiff’s land use, private nuisance applies.

Public Nuisance.rule: Public nuisance is an unreasonable interference with a right common to the general public.
buzzwords: right common to the general public
Public Nuisance.application: Because the conduct affected public rights, public nuisance applies.

Standing — Public Nuisance.rule: A private individual may recover for public nuisance only if the individual suffers a special injury different in kind from the general public.
Standing — Public Nuisance.application: Because the plaintiff suffered a special injury, standing exists.

Intentional Nuisance.rule: Intentional nuisance occurs when the defendant knows or is substantially certain that the conduct will result in interference with land use.
Intentional Nuisance.application: Because the defendant knew interference would occur, the nuisance was intentional.

Negligent Nuisance.rule: Negligent nuisance occurs when the defendant’s unreasonable interference results from negligent conduct.
Negligent Nuisance.application: Because the interference resulted from negligence, negligent nuisance applies.

Strict Liability Nuisance.rule: Strict liability nuisance occurs when interference results from abnormally dangerous activities.
Strict Liability Nuisance.application: Because the activity was abnormally dangerous, strict liability applies.

Unreasonableness — Balancing Test.rule: Unreasonableness in nuisance is determined by balancing the gravity of the harm against the utility of the defendant’s conduct.
Unreasonableness — Balancing Test.application: Because the harm outweighed the utility, the interference was unreasonable.

Coming to the Nuisance.rule: Coming to the nuisance is not a complete defense but may be considered in determining the reasonableness of the interference.
Coming to the Nuisance.application: Because the plaintiff came to the nuisance, it is considered but does not bar recovery.

Remedies — Nuisance.rule: Remedies for nuisance include damages, injunctions, or abatement depending on the circumstances.
Remedies — Nuisance.application: Because ongoing interference existed, an injunction was appropriate.
